__data__.text: Dasaratha blessed Rama, wishing him a long and prosperous reign with his brothers. Rama, in response, pleaded with his father to forgive Kaikeyi and Bharata, asking him to retract the curse he had placed upon them. Dasaratha agreed and then turned to Lakshmana, commending him for his devoted service to Rama and Sita.  He assured Lakshmana that this service would bring him immense merit, fame, and a place in heaven.  Dasaratha proclaimed Rama's divinity, stating that even the gods revered him as the Supreme Person, the unmanifest Brahma. He reiterated Lakshmana's virtuous service and the rewards it brought him. Finally, Dasaratha addressed Sita, advising her not to resent Rama's public test of her fidelity. He emphasized the difficulty of her ordeal and its power to eclipse the fame of other virtuous women.  While acknowledging Sita's inherent devotion, he reminded her that Rama should be considered her highest deity.  Having imparted his wisdom and blessings, Dasaratha departed for heaven in his celestial chariot.

Indra, though acknowledging the difficulty of the request, grants it, promising that the fallen warriors will rise again, healed and whole, to rejoin their families.

__data__.text: Indra assures Rama that his boon, though difficult, will be granted. The surrounding land will flourish with fruit and blossoms, and rivers will flow abundantly.  The monkeys rise, healed and whole, marveling at the miracle. The gods, witnessing Rama's success, urge him to return to Ayodhya, reassure Sita, see his brothers and mothers, and claim his throne. Indra and the other gods then depart for heaven. Rama and Lakshmana, having greeted the departing gods, then command the army to make camp. The vast army, shining brightly, resembles a moonlit night.

Puspaka, to take him to Ayodhya

__data__.text: After a restful night, Vibhishana addressed the victorious Rama, offering him various luxurious baths, clothes, ornaments, and garlands.  He presented skilled attendants to assist Rama with his bath.  However, Rama declined these offerings, stating his concern for his brother Bharata, who was suffering in Rama's absence.  Rama prioritized his return to Ayodhya over personal comforts, highlighting the difficulty of the journey on foot. Vibhishana reassured Rama, offering him the use of the celestial chariot Puspaka, which belonged to Kubera but had been taken by Ravana.  This magnificent chariot, resembling a cloud and capable of swift travel, would ensure a comfortable journey back to Ayodhya.  Vibhishana then implored Rama to stay a while in Lanka as a guest, accepting his hospitality along with Lakshmana and Sita, before departing.  He emphasized that this request stemmed from affection and respect, not a command.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto CXXI

Sri Rama prepares to leave for Ayodhya and at his instance
Vibhisana orders the presence of the aerial car,
Puspaka, to take him to Ayodhya

__data__.text: Vibhishana, deeply affectionate and respectful, implored Rama to stay longer in Lanka, offering lavish hospitality to him, Lakshmana, and Sita, before their departure. He assured Rama that this request was born of genuine care, not a command.  Rama, touched by Vibhishana's heartfelt offer, acknowledged the honor shown to him through Vibhishana's counsel, military support, and unwavering friendship.  However, Rama expressed his deep longing to return to Ayodhya and reunite with his anxious brother Bharata, his mothers, and his friend Guha, along with the people of his kingdom. He recounted Bharata's earlier plea for his return from Chitrakoota, a plea he had then denied.  Therefore, Rama requested Vibhishana to understand his urgency and grant him permission to leave. He asked for the Puspaka chariot to be prepared, emphasizing that his purpose in Lanka was fulfilled and his continued stay was no longer necessary.  Upon Rama's request, Vibhishana promptly summoned the magnificent aerial car, Puspaka, radiating like the sun.  The chariot was described as a marvel of artistry and luxury, with gold construction, cat's-eye gem seats, silver accents, ornate flags and banners, golden mansions, pearl-studded windows, and a network of melodious bells.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Upon Rama's request, Vibhishana swiftly summoned the magnificent aerial car, Puspaka, shining brightly like the sun. The chariot was a marvel of craftsmanship, adorned with gold and featuring seats of cat's-eye gems.  Its elegant design included ornate attics, silver accents, and decorative flags and banners. Golden mansions embellished with golden lotuses added to its splendor. Networks of small bells hung gracefully, and pearl-studded windows allowed light to filter through.  The melodious sound of bells surrounded the chariot.  Vibhishana then presented this extraordinary vehicle to Rama, which was crafted by the divine architect Viswakarma and resembled the peak of Mount Meru.  It boasted grand halls decorated with pearls and silver, along with crystal pavements and luxurious cat's-eye gem seats. The chariot, described as impregnable and as fast as thought, was ready for Rama's departure.  Rama and Lakshmana were astonished by the sight of the magnificent Puspaka, which could travel anywhere at will and resembled a towering mountain.

__data__.text: Vibhishana, having prepared the flower-adorned Puspaka, asked Rama for further instructions.  Rama instructed Vibhishana to honor the monkeys and bears who had fought valiantly. He emphasized their selfless dedication and bravery.  Rama further advised Vibhishana that generosity and compassion are essential qualities for a leader.  Following Rama's counsel, Vibhishana rewarded the monkeys with riches.  Witnessing this, Rama, along with Sita and Lakshmana, boarded the magnificent Puspaka.  From the aerial car, Rama addressed the monkeys, granting them permission to depart and praising Sugriva's loyalty and righteousness.

__data__.text: Having rewarded the monkey troops, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana boarded the magnificent Puspaka. Rama addressed the monkeys from the aerial car, granting them permission to return home. He praised Sugriva's loyalty and assured Vibhishana of his protection in Lanka. Rama then announced his intention to return to Ayodhya.  The monkeys and Vibhishana expressed their desire to accompany Rama.  Rama graciously agreed, promising a joyous reunion with his family and friends in Ayodhya.  At Rama's invitation, Sugriva, Vibhishana, their respective entourages, and the other monkeys boarded the Puspaka.  With everyone comfortably seated, the aerial car soared into the sky, carrying Rama, who shone like Kubera, and his companions.

With everyone settled, the magnificent aerial car, once belonging to Kubera, ascended into the sky with Rama's permission.  As the radiant Puspaka, adorned with a swan figurehead, glided through the air, Rama shone with a splendor akin to Kubera's. The powerful monkeys, bears, and ogres comfortably seated themselves within the spacious divine chariot.

to Sita the places over which they were flying

__data__.text: Rama, showing Sita the aerial view of Lanka from the Pushpaka Vimana, pointed out the devastation caused by the war.  He showed her the battlefield, a gruesome scene stained with the blood and remains of both monkeys and Rakshasas. He highlighted the fallen Ravana, killed by Rama himself to avenge Sita's abduction.  Rama then recounted the fates of other prominent Rakshasa warriors: Kumbhakarna, Prahasta, Dhumraksa, Vidyunmali, Indrajit, Vikata, Virupaksa, Mahaparshwa, Akampana, Trishira, Atikaya, Devantaka, Narantaka, Yuddhonmatta, Matta, Nikumbha, Kumbha, Vajradamstra, Darmstra, Makaraksa, Sonitaksa, Yupaksa, Prajangha, Vidyujjihva, Yajnasatru, and Suptaghna, all defeated by various members of Rama's army.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto CXXIII

In the course of their flight to Ayodhya, S11 Rama shows
to Sita the places over which they were flying

__data__.text: As they journeyed, Rama pointed out the sites of fallen Rakshasa warriors Akampana, Sonitaksa, Yupaksa, and Prajangha, as well as the fearsome Vidyujjihva, Yajnasatru, and Suptaghna.  He showed Sita where Mandodari mourned Ravana, surrounded by her fellow wives.  He indicated the spot where they had rested after crossing the ocean and the location of the bridge he built.  Rama then directed Sita's attention to the vast ocean and the golden Mount Mainaka, which had risen to offer Hanuman respite.  He showed her where his army had camped before crossing to Lanka and the place where Shiva had blessed him. He highlighted the sacred spot where the bridge construction began, a place of purification.  He also pointed out where Vibhishana first appeared.  Finally, Rama showed Sita the pleasant city of Kiskindha, where he had defeated Vali.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: As they flew, Rama showed Sita where Mandodari, surrounded by her fellow wives, mourned Ravana. He pointed out the spot on the seashore where they had rested after crossing the ocean and the location of the bridge he had built.  He directed Sita's gaze to the vast, seemingly boundless ocean and to Mount Mainaka, which had risen from the water to offer Hanuman a place to rest. He showed her his army's encampment before their crossing and the place where Shiva had blessed him before bridge construction began, a sacred site of purification.  He also indicated where Vibhishana first joined them.  Finally, he showed her the pleasant city of Kiskindha, where he had defeated Vali.

__data__.text: As they continued their journey, Rama pointed out the battlefield where he had vanquished Khara, Dusana, and the mighty Trisira. He then showed Sita their former hermitage, a picturesque hut amidst five banyan trees, the place from which Ravana had abducted her.  He directed her attention to the serene Godavari River and the nearby hermitages of the sages Agastya and Sutiksna, his disciple.  Finally, he pointed out the expansive hermitage of Sage Sarabhanga, a place Indra himself had visited.

Canto CXXIII

In the course of their flight to Ayodhya, S11 Rama shows
to Sita the places over which they were flying

__data__.text: As they flew, Rama showed Sita the hermitage of Sage Sarabhanga, which even Indra, king of the gods, had visited.  He pointed out the spot where he had slain the giant Viradha and the hermitage of Sage Atri, where Sita had met the virtuous Anasuya.  He showed her Citrakuta mountain, where Bharata had pleaded with him to return, and the lovely Yamuna River, along with the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja.  The holy Ganga River, teeming with life and bordered by blossoming trees, came into view.  He then indicated Srngaverapura, home to his friend Guha, and the Sarayu River, marked by remnants of ancient sacrifices. Finally, he pointed out Ayodhya, the capital city of his late father, and they both paid their respects.  The monkeys and Vibhishana eagerly looked down upon the city, admiring its pale mansions, wide streets, and the abundance of elephants and horses. It was a sight that rivaled even Indra's celestial city, Amaravati.

__data__.text: Upon reaching the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja, Rama respectfully greeted the sage and inquired about the well-being of Ayodhya, his brother Bharata, and his mothers.  The sage, Bharadwaja, happily reassured Rama that Bharata eagerly awaited his return, governing dutifully while keeping Rama's sandals as a symbol of his authority.  He recounted Rama's previous departure, acknowledging the prince's adherence to dharma and the hardships he endured in exile. Bharadwaja expressed joy at Rama's triumphant return, accompanied by his wife and brother, having achieved his purpose.  He revealed his awareness of Rama's trials and tribulations in the forest, including Sita's abduction by Ravana while Rama was protecting the ascetics. The sage detailed his knowledge of the events that followed, from Rama's alliance with Sugriva and the defeat of Vali, to the search for Sita, Hanuman's leap across the sea, the construction of the bridge to Lanka, the battle against Ravana, and Rama's eventual victory. Bharadwaja then offered Rama a blessing and invited him to stay.  Rama gratefully accepted the sage's hospitality and requested a boon: that the trees along his journey back to Ayodhya bear fruit and honey. The sage granted this wish, and the trees miraculously blossomed and bore fruit, providing sustenance for the monkeys who accompanied Rama.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Sage Bharadwaja recounted Rama's journey to the prince himself, demonstrating his encompassing knowledge gained through asceticism.  He detailed Rama's encounter with Kabandha, his arrival at Pampa lake, the alliance with Sugriva and the subsequent defeat of Vali.  The sage also described the search for Sita, Hanuman's courageous leap across the ocean, Nala's construction of the bridge to Lanka, and the joyous victory over Ravana’s forces, culminating in Ravana's death. He mentioned the meeting with the gods after Ravana’s defeat and the boons granted to Rama.  Bharadwaja then offered Rama a blessing and hospitality, prompting Rama to request that the trees along his journey back to Ayodhya bear fruit and honey. The sage granted this wish, transforming the trees into vibrant, fruit-laden versions of themselves, providing sustenance for Rama's monkey companions.

for the delightful news

__data__.text: Rama, eager to show kindness to his friends and the people of Ayodhya, instructed Hanuman to go to Ayodhya and ascertain the well-being of the people in the royal palace.  He then told Hanuman to proceed to Sringaverapura and inform Guha, the leader of the Nisadas, of Rama's well-being. Rama expressed his confidence that Guha, his dear friend, would be overjoyed by this news.  Rama further instructed Hanuman to convey his well-being to Bharata and inform him of Rama's successful return with Sita and Lakshmana.  Hanuman was also to relate the events of Sita's abduction by Ravana, Rama's alliance with Sugriva, the defeat of Vali, Hanuman's search for Sita, the building of the bridge across the ocean, and Ravana's eventual defeat. Finally, Hanuman was to tell Bharata of the boons bestowed upon Rama by Indra, Brahma, and Varuna, Rama's encounter with his deceased father, and his imminent arrival near Ayodhya with Vibhishana and Sugriva. Hanuman was instructed to observe Bharata's reaction to this news – his expression, gestures, and words.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto CXXV

Preceding Sri Rama to Srigaverapura and Nandigrama, Hanuman apprises
Guha and Bharata of Sri Rama’s impending return soon and Bharata
offers him a number of valuable gifts in recompense
for the delightful news

__data__.text: Hanuman informed Bharata of Rama's imminent arrival near Ayodhya, accompanied by Vibhishana and Sugriva. He relayed that Rama, having achieved victory and glory, was returning with powerful allies. Hanuman was instructed to observe Bharata's reaction to this news – his expression, demeanor, and words.  He was to discern whether Bharata's attachment to the kingdom had caused any desire for the throne.  Hanuman was urged to return quickly with this information. He then departed for Ayodhya with great speed, likened to Garuda pursuing a serpent.  After passing over the confluence of the Ganga and Yamuna rivers, he arrived at Sringaverapura and joyfully informed Guha of Rama's well-being and imminent arrival. He then swiftly departed again, eager to fulfill Rama's request. He told Guha that Rama would be visible at Sringaverapura after spending the night with Sage Bharadwaja. He also told Bharata that Rama had killed Ravana and recovered Sita, and was returning with his allies, including Lakshmana and a safe Sita.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto CXXV

Preceding Sri Rama to Srigaverapura and Nandigrama, Hanuman apprises

__data__.text: Hanuman, leaving Sringaverapura, assured Guha that Rama would arrive there the following day after staying the night with Sage Bharadwaja.  He then journeyed swiftly towards Ayodhya, passing various landmarks including rivers and a forest.  Near Nandigrama, he spotted Bharata, living austerely in a hermitage, clad in bark and deerskin, and deeply saddened by Rama's absence. Despite his grief, Bharata ruled righteously, surrounded by his ministers and advisors. Hanuman approached Bharata and informed him of Rama's return, sharing the news of Rama's victory over Ravana and Sita’s rescue.  Overjoyed by the news, Bharata embraced Hanuman, showering him with tears of joy and offering him a generous reward of cows, villages, and maidens.  Bharata, eager to see Rama, expressed his profound happiness at the news of his brother's return.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto CXXV

Preceding Sri Rama to Srigaverapura and Nandigrama, Hanuman apprises
Guha and Bharata of Sri Rama’s impending return soon and Bharata
offers him a number of valuable gifts in recompense
for the delightful news

__data__.text: Hanuman's journey continued towards Ayodhya. He passed sacred sites, rivers like Valukini, Vardthini, and Gomati, and a dense sal forest, encountering many people of Kosala.  Reaching the blossoming trees near Nandigrama, he saw Bharata living austerely in a hermitage two miles from Ayodhya.  Grief-stricken by Rama's absence, Bharata wore simple clothing, matted hair, and subsisted on fruits and roots. Despite his personal sorrow, he ruled justly, guided by ministers and advisors. Hanuman approached Bharata, who, though physically diminished, possessed a spiritual aura.  He announced Rama's imminent return after defeating Ravana and rescuing Sita, who was unharmed.  Overjoyed, Bharata embraced Hanuman, offering him a lavish reward of cows, villages, and women as a token of gratitude for the good news.  Bharata, filled with happiness and longing, eagerly awaited his brother's arrival.

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

__data__.text: Upon Bharata's inquiry, Hanuman began to recount Rama's journey.  He first reminded Bharata of the events leading to Rama's exile: the king's promise to Kaikeyi, Rama's departure, the king's death from grief, Bharata's return and refusal of the throne, his visit to Rama at Chitrakuta, and his return to Ayodhya with Rama's sandals.  Hanuman then described how Chitrakuta felt desolate after their departure.  Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana journeyed into the dense and dangerous Dandaka forest. There, they encountered the fearsome ogre Viradha, who attacked them. Rama and Lakshmana fought bravely, subdued Viradha, and cast him into a pit.  Following this, they reached the peaceful hermitage of Sage Sarabhanga.  After the sage's ascension to heaven, Rama and his companions continued their journey towards Janasthana.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

__data__.text: While journeying through the dense forest, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana encountered the ogre Viradha.  The brothers fought and trapped Viradha in a pit.  They then found respite at Sage Sarabhanga's hermitage. After the sage's heavenly ascension, Rama continued to Janasthana. There, Surpanakha approached Rama. Lakshmana intervened, resulting in a conflict where Rama defeated numerous ogres.  Surpanakha, seeking vengeance, went to Ravana.  Maricha, disguised as a beautiful deer, lured Rama away.  Ravana entered the hermitage while Lakshmana was also gone, and abducted Sita. Jatayu, the vulture king, tried to save Sita but was mortally wounded.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

__data__.text: Rama pursued and killed the deceptive deer.  Taking advantage of Rama and Lakshmana's absence, Ravana entered the hermitage and abducted Sita.  He carried her away in his aerial chariot, the Pushpaka, to Lanka, where he imprisoned her in a mansion.  Rama returned to find Sita and Jatayu, a vulture residing nearby, missing. He then discovered the slain Jatayu.  While searching for Sita, Rama and Lakshmana encountered and killed the ogre Kabandha. Following Kabandha's advice, they went to Mount Rsyamuka and met Sugriva, who had been exiled by his brother Vali.  A bond formed between Rama and Sugriva, and Rama helped Sugriva regain his kingdom by defeating Vali. In return, Sugriva promised to help Rama find Sita.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

__data__.text: Hanuman recounted Rama's journey after the abduction of Sita. Following Kabandha's guidance, Rama and Lakshmana allied with Sugriva on Mount Rsyamuka.  Rama assisted Sugriva in reclaiming his kingdom from Vali. In return, Sugriva pledged to locate Sita.  Sugriva dispatched a vast search party, including Hanuman, who, after a challenging search, located Sita in Ravana's captivity.  Hanuman delivered Rama's ring to Sita and returned with a jewel from her.  This proof of Sita's survival revived Rama's hope.  He then prepared for war against Ravana, building a bridge across the sea with Nala's help.  The monkey army crossed the sea, and key battles ensued: Nila defeated Prahasta, Rama defeated Kumbhakarna, Lakshmana defeated Indrajit, and Rama ultimately vanquished Ravana.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

Canto CXXVI

Hanuman recounts to Bharata broad details relating to the sojourn
of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana in the forest

__data__.text: Hanuman continued his account of Rama's exploits to Bharata.  With unwavering resolve, Rama focused on Lanka's destruction.  He had Nala build a bridge across the ocean, allowing the monkey army to cross.  Nila defeated Prahasta, Rama defeated Kumbhakarna, Lakshmana defeated Indrajit, and Rama finally triumphed over Ravana.  Blessed by Indra, Yama, Varuna, Shiva, Brahma, and the spirit of his father, Dasaratha, as well as by assembled sages and seers, Rama gratefully accepted their blessings.  He then returned to Kishkindha with his monkey army in the Pushpaka Vimana.  Hanuman assured Bharata that he could see Rama the next day, as Rama was staying near the Ganga river with Sage Bharadwaja.  Overjoyed at this news of Rama's imminent return, Bharata expressed his heartfelt joy and relief at the fulfillment of his long-held desire.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

it originally belonged

__data__.text: Upon hearing of Rama's imminent return, Bharata, a mighty warrior, joyfully instructed his brother Satrughna to prepare for the momentous occasion.  He commanded that the city's deities and temples be honored with fragrant flowers and music.  Bards, musicians, and citizens from all walks of life, including officials, soldiers, and commoners, were to assemble and welcome Rama.  Satrughna, following Bharata's orders, organized a large workforce to level the ground and beautify the route from Ayodhya to Nandigrama, sprinkling it with cool water and scattering flowers and grains.  He also instructed that the streets be lined with flags and the houses decorated with garlands and colorful ornaments.  A grand procession, led by the eight ministers atop elephants and accompanied by a multitude of warriors on horses, elephants, and chariots, set forth to greet Rama.

__data__.text: Hundreds of men lined the main road to ensure it remained clear for the procession.  Following Satrughna's command, the eight ministers, Dhrsti, Jayanta, Vijaya, Siddhartha, Arthasadhaka, Asoka, Mantrapala, and Sumantra, joyfully rode forth on elephants, followed by many more adorned elephants.  Other warriors rode on elephants, horses, and in chariots.  Champions were accompanied by numerous select horses, some of even higher quality, and by foot soldiers carrying various weapons.  Dasaratha's wives, led by Kausalya and Sumitra, traveled in palanquins to meet Rama.  Kaikeyi accompanied them to Nandigrama.  Bharata, surrounded by Brahmins, guild leaders, counselors bearing offerings, and cheered by musicians and praise singers, carried Rama's sandals and parasol.  Emaciated from fasting, clad in simple garments, and filled with joy, he went forth with his ministers.  The earth trembled under the sound of horses, chariots, conches, and drums.  Ayodhya itself seemed to have come to Nandigrama.  Bharata, surveying the scene, then addressed Hanuman. He expressed his hope that Hanuman, being a monkey, hadn't resorted to trickery, as he couldn't yet see Rama.

__data__.text: Bharata, still not seeing Rama, questioned Hanuman if he, being a monkey, had employed some trickery. Hanuman reassured Bharata, pointing out the sounds of joyful monkeys and bears feasting on fruit-laden trees, a blessing bestowed by Sage Bharadwaja and Indra.  He directed Bharata's attention to a rising dust cloud near the sal trees, indicating the approaching monkey army crossing the Gomati River.  Hanuman then described Rama's approach in the celestial, moon-like Pushpaka Vimana, obtained after defeating Ravana. He highlighted the vimana's creation by Vishwakarma and its radiant, sun-like brilliance.  He identified the occupants: Rama, Lakshmana, Sita, Sugriva, and Vibhishana.  As Hanuman announced Rama's arrival, a joyous clamor erupted.  The people, dismounting their chariots, elephants, and horses, gazed at Rama in the vimana.  A joyful Bharata, with folded hands, reverently greeted and worshipped Rama from afar with symbolic offerings.  Rama, seated in the vimana, shone like Indra.  Bharata bowed deeply to his elder brother.  With Rama's permission, the magnificent, swift Pushpaka Vimana descended to the ground.

__data__.text: Upon Rama's arrival, a joyous Bharata reverently worshipped him from afar with traditional offerings.  Rama, seated in the Pushpaka Vimana, radiated a divine aura.  Bharata then bowed deeply to his elder brother. With Rama's permission, the magnificent vimana descended.  Overjoyed, Bharata ascended the vimana and greeted Rama once more.  Rama embraced Bharata, who had been absent for so long.  Bharata then greeted Lakshmana and Sita, acknowledging her by name. He warmly embraced Sugriva, Jambavan, Angada, Mainda, Dwivida, Nila, and Rsabha.  He also embraced Sushena, Nala, Gavaksha, Gandhamadana, Sarabha, and Panasa. The monkeys, having assumed human form, joyfully inquired about Bharata's well-being.  Bharata, embracing Sugriva, declared him a brother, emphasizing that friendship equates to brotherhood and ill will defines an enemy.  He commended Vibhishana for his crucial role in their victory.  Satrughna then greeted Rama, Lakshmana, and respectfully bowed to Sita. Finally, Rama approached his mother Kausalya, who was pale and weakened from grief, and reverently touched her feet, bringing her immense joy. He then greeted Sumitra, Kaikeyi, his other mothers, and Sage Vasistha.

__data__.text: Bharata expressed his admiration for Vibhishana's crucial alliance with Rama, acknowledging the difficulty of their achieved victory.  Satrughna then greeted Rama and Lakshmana, and respectfully bowed to Sita. Rama approached his mother Kausalya, who appeared pale and weakened by grief, and touched her feet, bringing her comfort. He then greeted Sumitra, Kaikeyi, and the other mothers, along with Sage Vasistha. The citizens of Ayodhya welcomed Rama, expressing their joy at his return. Rama observed their upraised hands, a gesture of reverence. Bharata, retrieving Rama's sandals, placed them at his brother's feet.  He then formally returned the kingdom to Rama, expressing his fulfillment in witnessing Rama's return.  Bharata encouraged Rama to inspect the kingdom's resources, which had been diligently managed in his absence. Witnessing this brotherly affection, both the monkeys and Vibhishana were moved to tears. Rama then carried Bharata in the Pushpaka Vimana to Bharata's hermitage. Upon arrival, Rama disembarked from the vimana and granted permission for the divine vehicle to return to its rightful owner, Kubera.  The Pushpaka Vimana then departed northward towards Kubera's abode.

He then addressed the magnificent aerial car, granting it permission to return to its rightful owner, Kubera.  The Pushpaka Vimana, once held by Ravana, then flew northwards towards Kubera's celestial abode.  Afterwards, Rama respectfully greeted Sage Vasistha, his family priest and advisor, and sat beside him on a prepared seat.

Canto CXXVIII

__data__.text: Bharata humbly returned the kingdom to Rama, stating his inability to bear the burden of governance. He compared himself to a young ox struggling under a load meant for a strong bull, and the kingdom to a leaking dam susceptible to the onslaught of challenges.  Bharata asserted his inability to follow in Rama's footsteps, likening himself to a donkey trying to match a horse's gallop or a crow mimicking a swan's flight. He used the analogy of a fruitless tree to emphasize the importance of Rama's rule, urging him to accept the throne for the benefit of his people.  Bharata requested Rama to accept the kingship and let the world witness his glory.  He wished for Rama to experience the joys of kingship, surrounded by music and festivities, and to rule the world as long as the earth and heavens exist.  Accepting Bharata's plea, Rama sat upon a magnificent throne. At Shatrughna's command, skilled barbers attended to Rama. After Bharata, Lakshmana, Sugriva, and Vibhishana had bathed, Rama, with his matted locks untangled, bathed, adorned with garlands and fragrant pastes, and dressed in fine attire, shone with radiant splendor.

__data__.text: Upon Bharata's submission, Rama ascended a magnificent throne.  At Shatrughna's behest, skilled barbers attended to Rama.  First, Bharata, Lakshmana, Sugriva, and Vibhishana bathed. Then, with his matted locks untangled and adorned with garlands and fragrant pastes, Rama, dressed in fine attire, appeared radiant.  Shatrughna oversaw the adornment of Rama and Lakshmana with jewels.  The widows of King Dasaratha adorned Sita.  Kausalya, overjoyed by the return of her family, adorned the wives of the monkey warriors.  Sumantra, the charioteer-minister, presented a splendid chariot to Rama at Shatrughna's command.  Rama, seeing the chariot, mounted it.  Sugriva and Hanuman, resplendent in celestial robes and earrings, proceeded towards the city.  Sugriva's wives, adorned with jewels and eager to see Ayodhya, accompanied Sita. Back in Ayodhya, King Dasaratha's ministers, led by Sage Vasistha, deliberated on Rama's coronation. Ministers Asoka, Vijaya, and Siddhartha consulted together for Rama's advancement and Ayodhya's prosperity. They instructed the servants to prepare meticulously for Rama's coronation. Following these instructions, the ministers and the priest departed to see Rama. Rama, in his chariot, proceeded towards Ayodhya, much like Indra in his chariot drawn by green horses.

__data__.text: Ministers Asoka, Vijaya, and Siddhartha diligently prepared for Rama's coronation, instructing the servants to make ready for the momentous occasion.  The ministers and priest then departed to greet Rama.  Rama, majestic in his chariot, journeyed towards Ayodhya, reminiscent of Indra. Bharata drove the chariot, Shatrughna held the parasol, and Lakshmana fanned Rama. Vibhishana stood on the other side, also fanning Rama.  Sages and gods sang praises as Rama approached. Sugriva, mounted on a large elephant, followed, accompanied by nine thousand monkeys adorned in human form and jewels.  Amidst joyous sounds of conches, drums, and cheers, Rama entered the city.  The people of Ayodhya welcomed Rama, who, surrounded by his brothers, radiated splendor. Ministers, Brahmins, and citizens surrounded him as he processed, shining brightly like the moon among stars. Musicians played festive songs as they led the procession. Offerings of rice, cows, maidens, Brahmins, and sweets were presented before Rama.  Rama then spoke to his advisors about his alliance with Sugriva, Hanuman's strength, and the monkeys' contributions.

__data__.text: Rama, surrounded by his ministers, Brahmins, and the people of Ayodhya, shone brightly like the moon amongst the stars.  Musicians with trumpets, cymbals, and other instruments played joyous songs as Rama proceeded through the city.  Offerings of rice, cows, maidens, Brahmins, and sweets were presented to him.  Rama then spoke with his advisors about his alliance with Sugriva, Hanuman's power, the monkeys' accomplishments, and his meeting with Vibhishana. The citizens of Ayodhya were amazed to hear of these events.  Rama, accompanied by the monkeys, entered the city, which was filled with happy people.  The citizens raised banners on their homes. Rama arrived at his father's palace. He greeted his mothers, Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi.  Rama then instructed Bharata to offer his palace and the Asoka grove to Sugriva. Bharata led Sugriva into the palace, and servants brought in lamps and bedding.  Bharata instructed Sugriva to send messengers to collect seawater from the four seas for Rama's coronation. Sugriva gave golden, jewel-encrusted pails to four monkey chiefs and told them to return with the seawater by the next morning.  The mighty monkeys then soared into the sky like eagles.

__data__.text: Bharata urged Sugriva to send messengers for seawater to be used in Rama's coronation ceremony.  Sugriva promptly provided four monkey chiefs with ornate golden pails and instructed them to return by the next morning with water from the four seas. The mighty monkeys then took flight. Jambavan, Hanuman, Gavaya, and Rsabha brought seawater, while other monkeys brought water from various rivers. Jambavan brought water from the eastern sea, Rsabha from the southern sea, Gavaya from the western ocean, and Hanuman from the northern sea.  Satrughna, along with his ministers, presented the collected water to Sage Vasistha and others for Rama's coronation.  Sage Vasistha then guided Rama and Sita to their designated seats. Several sages performed the consecration ritual, using pure, fragrant water.  Brahmins, virgins, ministers, warriors, and merchants also participated in the ceremony. Finally, the gods, including the four guardians of the world, sprinkled Rama with the essence of medicinal herbs.

Canto CXXVIII

__data__.text: After the initial consecration, sages Vasistha, Vamadeva, Jabali, Kasyapa, Katyayana, Suyajna, Gautama, and Vijaya continued the ceremony, using pure, fragrant water, reminiscent of the Vasus consecrating Indra.  Brahmins, sixteen virgins, ministers, joyful warriors, and merchants then participated in the ritual.  The gods, including the four guardians of the world, also blessed Rama by sprinkling him with the essence of medicinal herbs.  Rama was crowned with a magnificent, gem-studded crown crafted by Brahma, a crown previously worn by Manu and subsequent kings during their coronations.  Satrughna held the royal parasol, while Sugriva and Vibhishana held whisks. The wind-god, at Indra's behest, gifted Rama a golden lotus garland and a pearl necklace adorned with various jewels.  Celestial musicians and nymphs celebrated the occasion with song and dance.  The land flourished with crops, fruit-laden trees, and fragrant flowers. Rama generously gifted horses, cows, bulls, and vast amounts of gold to the Brahmins. He also bestowed a radiant, gem-encrusted garland upon Sugriva and exquisite armlets upon Angada.  He gave Sita a magnificent pearl necklace that shimmered like moonbeams, along with pristine robes and other jewels. Sita, touched by Hanuman's dedication, offered him her own pearl necklace.  Observing her intent, Rama encouraged her to bestow the gift upon Hanuman, who then shone brightly, adorned with the necklace. Rama honored Vibhishana, Sugriva, Hanuman, Jambavan, and other monkey chiefs with gifts befitting their contributions.  He also presented Dwivida, Mainda, and Nila with valuable items.  Content with their rewards, the monkeys returned home.

Indra prompted the wind-god to gift Rama a golden lotus garland and a pearl necklace adorned with jewels. Celestial musicians and nymphs celebrated Rama's consecration. The land prospered with crops, fruits, and fragrant flowers. Rama generously gifted horses, cows, bulls, and gold to the Brahmins. He gave Sugriva a radiant garland, Angada exquisite armlets, and Sita a magnificent pearl necklace, along with fine robes and jewels. __data__.text: Rama generously bestowed gifts upon the monkeys, honoring their service.  Elders and prominent figures like Vibhishana, Sugriva, Hanuman, and Jambavan received fitting rewards.  Content with their honors, the monkey leaders returned home.  Rama also gifted Dwivida, Mainda, and Nila before they departed for Kishkindha.  Sugriva, having witnessed Rama's coronation, returned to his city.  Vibhishana, now king of Lanka, returned with his followers.  Rama, the righteous and renowned king, ruled Kosala with joy and asked Lakshmana to be his regent. When Lakshmana declined, Rama appointed Bharata instead.  Rama performed numerous sacrifices, including the Paundarika, Ashwamedha, and Vajapeya. He ruled for over ten thousand years, conducting a hundred Ashwamedha sacrifices.  During Rama's reign, people lived long, healthy lives, blessed with many children.  The world seemed to revolve around Rama, and nature flourished.  The social classes lived harmoniously, content with their respective duties, free from greed.  People were truthful, virtuous, and blessed with auspicious marks.  Rama ruled with his brothers for 11,000 years.

__data__.text: Rama, strong and glorious, ruled the earth with Lakshmana by his side.  He governed righteously, conducting many sacrifices with his family and friends. During his reign, peace prevailed.  There were no widows, no threats from animals, and no diseases.  Crime vanished, and the elderly did not outlive the young.  Everyone was joyful and righteous, focused on Rama, and refrained from harming each other.  People lived long, healthy lives, blessed with many children.  Conversation centered around Rama, and the world seemed to embody him.  Nature thrived, with trees constantly bearing fruit and flowers, rain falling as needed, and gentle breezes.  The social classes lived in harmony, content with their work and free from greed.  Everyone was honest, virtuous, and blessed.  Rama ruled with his brothers for 11,000 years.  Listening to this epic, composed by Valmiki, brings merit, fame, long life, and victory. It also fulfills desires for children and wealth.  Kings who hear it conquer their foes. The gods are pleased by those who listen, and obstacles are removed for those who possess a copy.  Listening brings success to kings, safe travels to wanderers, and exceptional sons to expectant mothers. Reading and worshipping this text eliminates sins and grants long life. Kshatriyas should hear it daily from Brahmins, which will ensure their prosperity and progeny. Daily reading or listening pleases Rama, who is the eternal Vishnu. This ancient narrative brings blessings.  Sharing it increases Vishnu's power.  The gods and ancestors are gratified by hearing it. Those who transcribe it are destined for heaven.

__data__.text: This section of the Ramayana extols the virtues of hearing and reading the epic.  Listening to Valmiki's ancient poem bestows religious merit, fame, long life, and victory, especially for kings.  It removes sins, grants desires for children and wealth, and helps overcome difficulties.  Women who listen will be blessed with sons and grandsons.  The gods, including Vishnu (as Rama), are pleased by those who hear the epic, and obstacles are removed for those who possess a copy.  Kings find success, travelers safety, and expectant mothers are blessed with exceptional sons.  Reading and worshipping the text eliminates sins and grants long life.  Kshatriyas, especially, should hear it daily from Brahmins to ensure prosperity and progeny.  Rama, the eternal Vishnu, is pleased by daily readings or listenings.  The narrative increases Vishnu's power and gratifies the gods and ancestors.  Those who transcribe it are destined for heaven.  The story also emphasizes Rama's lineage, with Lakshmana identified as Sesa, the serpent god.  The narrative is described as beneficial for longevity, health, fame, fraternal affection, wisdom, vitality, and prosperity.

Canto XL

Ascending the highest peak of the Suvela mountain alongwith a division
of his army, Sri Rama catches sight of Ravana perched on the top
of a gate of his palace. Infuriated at his very sight, Sugriva
springs upon him by surprise from the mountain-top,
throws down the diadem from his head, struggles
with him for a long time and, eluding
him, returns victorious to the
presence of Sri Rama

Canto XLI

Sri Rama is not very pleased with the daring act of Sugriva inasmuch as he
had seen evil portents. Warning him against such action on the ground
of his being a sovereign, and commanding the monkey generals to
besiege all the four gates of Lanka, he dispatches Angada to
expostulate with Ravana and bring him to reason. Angada
tries his best to bring him round, but in vain, and
returns unsuccessful to the presence of Sri
Rama after threatening the ogres

Canto XLII

Approaching Ravana, the ogres break to him the news about Lanka having
been laid siege to by the monkeys led by Sri Rama. Ascending the roof
of his palace and taxing his brain about the means of putting an end
to the monkeys, he casts a look at Sri Rama and the monkeys.
Pained at the thought that Sita was being bullied by Ravana
on his account, and commanding the monkeys to besiege
all the four gates of Lanka and to destroy the ogres,

Sri Rama, on the other side, takes up his position
alongwith Laksmana near the northern gate.

Apprised of these developments, Ravana
mobilizes his army and, encouraged
by him, the ogres beat their drums
and emit a terrible roar

Canto XLIV

During the nocturnal engagement the monkeys too dauntlessly offer a stiff
fight to the ogres even though they were fighting against heavy
odds inasmuch as the strength of ogres gets doubled at night,
and felt highly rejoiced to find Indrajit vanquished by
Angada. Sri Rama and Laksmana too put to flight
numberless foes with their shower of arrows.
Eventually, however, they are both
bound by Indrajit in a
noose of serpents

Canto XLVI

Though itching to killl the enemy on beholding Sri Rama and
Laksmana enmeshed in a network of snakes, the monkeys,
who were unable to perceive him, feel frustrated.
Vibhisana exhorts Sugriva, who was seized
with fear on finding Indrajit hidden under
the cloak of witchcraft, to take heart and
not to give way to fear. Concluding
the two brothers to be dead, Indrajit,
for his part, triumphantly

enters Lanka and is
welcomed by
his father

Canto XLVIII

“Those well-versed in physiognomy and palmistry had prophesied
that I would never be widowed and would bear sons. How
could their predictions prove untrue?” While Sita was
absorbed in these thoughts on beholding Sri
Rama and Laksmana, Trijata reassures
her on the strength of good omens
and, cheering her soul, takes her
back to the Asoka grove

Canto LI

Hearing the tumultuous noise of the monkeys, the servants of Ravana,
detailed for the purpose, report to him how the two scions of
Raghu were freed from the bondage of arrows. Depressed
by this news, Ravana dispatches Dhimraksa to dispose
of Sri Rama. Though perturbed at the sight of evil
portents, yet unwilling to retrace his steps
on any account, Dhumraksa surveys
the army of the monkeys
